topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias had spent forty years coaxing life from clay, the rhythmic kneading a balm to his soul. His workshop smelled perpetually of damp earth and fired minerals. Then came Leo’s introduction to pottery. At first, it was clumsy enthusiasm—smeared hands, lopsided bowls. But now, Leo treated the wheel like a sacred altar. He demanded perfection, obsessive symmetry in every curve. Elias watched from the doorway, his knuckles white where he gripped the frame. Each time Leo finished a piece, the silence in the room stretched thin, taut as a drying clay wall. When Leo showed him the latest creation—a vase with impossibly thin, spiraling lines—Elias felt a cold knot tighten beneath his ribs. He kept glancing at his own tools, suddenly feeling clumsy, inadequate, as if his lifelong mastery was suddenly obsolete in the face of his son’s fervent, consuming devotion.

[story 2]

"Can I try the glaze, Dad?" Leo asked, his voice vibrating with an intensity that made the hairs on my arms stand up. I was wiping down my workbench, trying to focus on the mundane task of cleaning residue, but my attention was snagged on the small, perfectly formed figurine Leo had left on the shelf. It was a miniature rendition of my favorite kiln. He hadn't just copied it; he’d painstakingly replicated the firing patterns, using specialized tools I didn't recognize. A tremor ran through my hand as I reached for it. It wasn't admiration I felt, but a sharp, unwelcome sense of intrusion. I retreated to the back room, the heavy door clicking shut behind me. I found myself staring at the stack of antique bisque ware, feeling a sudden, desperate need to hide the evidence of my own solitary pursuit.

[story 3]

The sound of water running from the tap was the only thing that broke the tension in the kitchen. I watched Leo, perched on a stool, meticulously cataloging the tiny shards of porcelain we’d broken yesterday. He wasn't playing; he was studying. He held a chipped rim up to the light, his brow furrowed in deep concentration. He spoke about kiln temperatures and clay composition with a frightening, technical fluency that belied his age. "It has to be exactly 1240 degrees Celsius for the right tensile strength," he murmured, his eyes shining with a fierce, unwavering light. I felt a strange hollowness bloom in my chest. My hobby had always been about patient, intuitive creation; his was about precise, almost clinical replication. Every time he spoke of the material, it felt less like shared interest and more like a proprietary claim. I cleared my throat, the sound scratchy in the quiet room, and felt a distinct urge to walk away from the scene before I said something that might shatter the fragile, perfect world he was building around himself.
